schools.summary: Joseph's grief over his dead father: 'and Joseph fell upon his father's face, and wept upon him, and kissed him' — Joseph, at Jacob's death (49:33), falling on his father's face, weeping and kissing him (the deep grief of the loving son, the tender mourning, the affection of Joseph for the father he had so lately regained, 46:29); Joseph's weeping (the natural, lawful grief, even of the godly, at the death of a beloved father, the tears of true affection, as Jesus wept, John 11:35, the mourning not forbidden, but the excess to be moderated by faith); 'and kissed him' (Joseph's last kiss on the dead father, the tender farewell, the closing of Jacob's eyes by Joseph, as promised, 46:4, the love of the son, the honor to the father at his death, the godly grief) — Gill, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Joseph fell upon his father's face, and wept upon him, and kissed him
Joseph's grief over his dead father
the deep, lawful grief of the loving son (49:33; John 11:35)
the tender farewell, the closing of Jacob's eyes (46:4)

schools.summary: Jacob embalmed after the Egyptian custom: 'and Joseph commanded his servants the physicians to embalm his father: and the physicians embalmed Israel' — Joseph having Jacob embalmed (the Egyptian art of preserving the dead, by which the body was kept from corruption, the physicians, the embalmers, doing the work, the Egyptian custom followed, for the long journey to Canaan, vv.7-13, and the honor of the patriarch); the embalming (the Egyptian practice, well-known, the body preserved with spices and natron) — the custom of the land (the honor done to Jacob, the practical necessity, for the journey to the burial in Canaan); 'the physicians embalmed Israel' (Jacob, Israel, embalmed, the honor of the patriarch, like the embalming of Joseph himself, v.26, the body preserved, the providence, the embalmed body to be carried to Canaan, vv.7-13, the faith that buried in the promised land) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Joseph commanded his servants the physicians to embalm his father: and the physicians embalmed Israel
Jacob embalmed after the Egyptian custom
the Egyptian art preserving the patriarch (as Joseph, v.26)
for the journey to the burial in Canaan (vv.7-13)

schools.summary: The mourning for Jacob: 'and forty days were fulfilled for him; for so are fulfilled the days of those which are embalmed: and the Egyptians mourned for him threescore and ten days' — the embalming taking forty days (the Egyptian period), and the Egyptians mourning Jacob seventy days (the period of mourning, nearly that of a king, the honor done to Jacob for Joseph's sake, the great mourning, the honor of the patriarch); the forty days of embalming (the Egyptian art, the time it took) — the preparing of the body (the custom); 'the Egyptians mourned for him threescore and ten days' (the seventy days of mourning, the honor, nearly royal, done to Jacob for Joseph's sake, the great esteem, the whole land mourning the father of the governor, the honor to the patriarch, the providence exalting the covenant family even in death) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And forty days were fulfilled for him... and the Egyptians mourned for him threescore and ten days
the mourning for Jacob
forty days embalmed, seventy days mourned
the nearly-royal honor done to Jacob for Joseph's sake

schools.summary: Joseph seeking leave to bury Jacob in Canaan: 'and when the days of his mourning were past, Joseph spake unto the house of Pharaoh, saying, If now I have found grace in your eyes, speak, I pray you, in the ears of Pharaoh, saying' — Joseph, the mourning past, asking the courtiers to intercede with Pharaoh for leave to go bury his father in Canaan (the courteous request, Joseph, though governor, seeking leave, perhaps because, as a mourner, he did not appear before Pharaoh directly, or out of deference, the seeking of permission); Joseph's courteous, humble request (through the courtiers) — the deference (the seeking of leave, for the journey to Canaan, vv.5-6); 'speak, I pray you, in the ears of Pharaoh' (Joseph asking the courtiers to bring his request before Pharaoh, the seeking of leave to bury Jacob in Canaan, the oath, v.5, the providence, the burial in the promised land) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And when the days of his mourning were past, Joseph spake unto the house of Pharaoh... speak, I pray you, in the ears of Pharaoh
Joseph seeking leave to bury Jacob in Canaan
the courteous, deferential request through the courtiers
the seeking of leave for the burial in the promised land (v.5)

schools.summary: Joseph citing Jacob's oath to bury him in Canaan: 'my father made me swear, saying, Lo, I die: in my grave which I have digged for me in the land of Canaan, there shalt thou bury me. Now therefore let me go up, I pray thee, and bury my father, and I will come again' — Joseph citing Jacob's oath (47:29-31): the dying father had made Joseph swear to bury him in Canaan (in the grave he had prepared, the cave of Machpelah, 49:29-31, the binding oath, the reason for the journey, the burial in the promised land); the grave (the cave of Machpelah, 49:30, bought by Abraham, 23:16-20, where the patriarchs were buried) — Jacob's chosen burial-place (the family sepulchre, the burial in the covenant land); 'let me go up... and bury my father, and I will come again' (Joseph asking leave, citing the oath as his warrant, promising to return, the faith that buried in Canaan, not Egypt, the dying patriarch looking to the promised land, Hebrews 11:13-22, the providence, the burial in the land of promise) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: My father made me swear... in my grave which I have digged for me in the land of Canaan, there shalt thou bury me... let me go up... and bury my father
Joseph citing Jacob's oath to bury him in Canaan
the binding oath (47:29-31), the grave at Machpelah (49:30; 23:16-20)
the faith that buried in the promised land, not Egypt (Hebrews 11:13-22)

schools.summary: Pharaoh granting leave for the burial: 'and Pharaoh said, Go up, and bury thy father, according as he made thee swear' — Pharaoh granting Joseph leave to go bury Jacob in Canaan, in keeping with the oath (the king's ready permission, the honor to Joseph, and to Jacob, the leave given for the journey, vv.7-13); Pharaoh's leave (the ready grant) — the honor to Joseph (the permission for the burial, the king's favor); 'according as he made thee swear' (Pharaoh honoring the oath, the binding vow, the leave to fulfill it, the providence, the burial in Canaan, the faith of the patriarchs honored even by the heathen king) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Pharaoh said, Go up, and bury thy father, according as he made thee swear
Pharaoh granting leave for the burial
the king's ready permission and favor
the oath honored, the burial in Canaan granted even by the heathen king

schools.summary: The great company going up to bury Jacob: 'and Joseph went up to bury his father: and with him went up all the servants of Pharaoh, the elders of his house, and all the elders of the land of Egypt' — Joseph going up to Canaan to bury Jacob, accompanied by a great company: the servants of Pharaoh, the elders of his house, and the elders of Egypt (the great funeral procession, the honor done to Jacob, the dignitaries of Egypt attending); the great train (the court and the elders of Egypt) — the honor to Joseph and to Jacob (the dignity of the procession, the esteem of the patriarch); 'all the elders of the land of Egypt' (the chief men of Egypt accompanying, the great honor, the providence, the covenant patriarch honored by the nation, the dignity of the burial, the faith that buried in Canaan, attended by the great) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, JFB, Calvin.

themes: And Joseph went up to bury his father: and with him went up all the servants of Pharaoh, the elders of his house, and all the elders of the land of Egypt
the great company going up to bury Jacob
the great funeral procession, the dignitaries of Egypt attending
the covenant patriarch honored by the nation

schools.summary: Jacob's family joining the procession, leaving the little ones in Goshen: 'and all the house of Joseph, and his brethren, and his father's house: only their little ones, and their flocks, and their herds, they left in the land of Goshen' — the whole family of Jacob (Joseph's house, his brethren, his father's house) joining the procession to Canaan, only the little children and the flocks and herds left in Goshen (as a pledge of return, and for the care of the young and the cattle, the family attending the burial); the whole family joining (the covenant household honoring the patriarch) — the family procession (the burial attended by all); 'only their little ones, and their flocks, and their herds, they left in the land of Goshen' (the children and cattle left behind, a pledge of their return to Egypt, vv.14, and the care of the young, the providence, the family to return to Goshen until the Exodus) — Gill.

themes: And all the house of Joseph, and his brethren, and his father's house: only their little ones, and their flocks, and their herds, they left in the land of Goshen
Jacob's family joining the procession, leaving the little ones in Goshen
the whole covenant household honoring the patriarch
the young left as a pledge of return to Egypt (v.14)

schools.summary: The very great company with chariots and horsemen: 'and there went up with him both chariots and horsemen: and it was a very great company' — the procession including chariots and horsemen (a military escort, for the long journey and the honor, the very great company, the magnitude of the funeral train, the honor done to Jacob, the dignity of the procession, befitting the father of the governor of Egypt); the chariots and horsemen (the escort, for safety on the journey, and for the state of the procession) — the dignity (the military honor); 'it was a very great company' (the great train, the court, the elders, the family, the escort, the magnitude of the honor, the providence, the covenant patriarch borne to Canaan with great pomp, the burial attended by the great) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And there went up with him both chariots and horsemen: and it was a very great company
the very great company with chariots and horsemen
the military escort and the dignity of the procession
the patriarch borne to Canaan with great pomp

schools.summary: The great mourning at the threshingfloor of Atad: 'and they came to the threshingfloor of Atad, which is beyond Jordan, and there they mourned with a great and very sore lamentation: and he made a mourning for his father seven days' — the company coming to Atad (a threshingfloor, beyond, i.e., on the way to, Jordan) and there holding a great mourning (the solemn lamentation, the place renamed for the mourning, Abel-mizraim, v.11, the great grief); the great mourning (the lamentation, the funeral rites, with the loud Eastern grief) — the solemn mourning for Jacob (the depth of the grief, the honor to the patriarch); 'he made a mourning for his father seven days' (Joseph's seven days of mourning, the customary period, as for Saul, 1 Samuel 31:13, the formal lamentation, the honor to Jacob, the providence, the patriarch mourned with great honor on the way to the burial in Canaan) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And they came to the threshingfloor of Atad, which is beyond Jordan, and there they mourned with a great and very sore lamentation: and he made a mourning for his father seven days
the great mourning at the threshingfloor of Atad
the solemn lamentation for Jacob, seven days (as 1 Samuel 31:13)
the honor and grief on the way to the burial

schools.summary: The place named Abel-mizraim for the mourning: 'and when the inhabitants of the land, the Canaanites, saw the mourning in the floor of Atad, they said, This is a grievous mourning to the Egyptians: wherefore the name of it was called Abelmizraim, which is beyond Jordan' — the Canaanites, seeing the great mourning (the Egyptian company lamenting), saying it was a grievous mourning of the Egyptians (the place named Abel-mizraim, 'the mourning of the Egyptians,' the memorial of the great lamentation, the renaming for the event); the Canaanites' remark (the great mourning of the Egyptians, observed by the people of the land) — the impression made by the solemn lamentation (the witness of the grief); 'the name of it was called Abelmizraim' (the place named, Abel-mizraim, the mourning of the Egyptians, the memorial of the event, a play on Abel, 'mourning,' and 'meadow,' the lasting name, the honor to Jacob remembered) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And when the inhabitants of the land, the Canaanites, saw the mourning in the floor of Atad... the name of it was called Abelmizraim
the place named Abel-mizraim for the mourning
the Canaanites observing the grievous mourning of the Egyptians
the lasting memorial of the great lamentation for Jacob

schools.summary: Jacob's sons fulfilling his burial charge: 'and his sons did unto him according as he commanded them' — Jacob's sons carrying out his charge concerning his burial (49:29-32): to bury him in the cave of Machpelah in Canaan (the obedience of the sons, the fulfilling of the dying father's command, the honor to Jacob); the sons' obedience to Jacob's charge (49:29-32) — the carrying out of the burial command (the dutiful sons, the honor to the father); the sons fulfilling the charge (the burial in Machpelah, v.13) — the obedience to the dying father (the burial in the covenant land, the faith of the patriarchs, Hebrews 11:13-22, the providence, the patriarch laid with his fathers) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And his sons did unto him according as he commanded them
Jacob's sons fulfilling his burial charge
the obedience to the dying father's charge (49:29-32)
the burial in the covenant land (Hebrews 11:13-22)

schools.summary: Jacob buried in the cave of Machpelah: 'for his sons carried him into the land of Canaan, and buried him in the cave of the field of Machpelah, which Abraham bought with the field for a possession of a buryingplace of Ephron the Hittite, before Mamre' — Jacob's sons carrying his body to Canaan and burying him in the cave of Machpelah (the family sepulchre, the burial in the covenant land, the patriarch laid with his fathers, Abraham, Isaac, Sarah, Rebekah, Leah, 49:31); the cave of Machpelah (bought by Abraham, 23:16-20, for a burying-place) — the family tomb (the only possession the patriarchs held in the promised land, a pledge of the inheritance, Hebrews 11:13, the burial-place of the fathers); 'before Mamre' (the place, near Hebron, 23:19, the family sepulchre, the burial of Jacob with his fathers, the faith that buried in Canaan, looking for the promised inheritance, Hebrews 11:13-22, the patriarch laid in the covenant land) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB.

themes: For his sons carried him into the land of Canaan, and buried him in the cave of the field of Machpelah, which Abraham bought... before Mamre
Jacob buried in the cave of Machpelah
the patriarch laid with his fathers in the family sepulchre (49:31; 23:16-20)
the burial in the covenant land, a pledge of the inheritance (Hebrews 11:13-22)

schools.summary: The company returning to Egypt after the burial: 'and Joseph returned into Egypt, he, and his brethren, and all that went up with him to bury his father, after he had buried his father' — Joseph and the company returning to Egypt after burying Jacob (the keeping of the promise, to return, v.5, the going back to Goshen, where the little ones were, v.8, the resuming of the sojourn, until the Exodus); Joseph's return (the promise kept, v.5) — the going back to Egypt (the resuming of his office, the sojourn continued); 'after he had buried his father' (the burial accomplished, the oath fulfilled, v.5, the return to Egypt, the providence, the family to remain in Egypt until the Exodus, the burial in Canaan a pledge of the future return) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Joseph returned into Egypt, he, and his brethren, and all that went up with him to bury his father, after he had buried his father
the company returning to Egypt after the burial
the promise to return kept, the oath fulfilled (v.5)
the sojourn resumed until the Exodus

schools.summary: The brethren's fear of Joseph's revenge: 'and when Joseph's brethren saw that their father was dead, they said, Joseph will peradventure hate us, and will certainly requite us all the evil which we did unto him' — the brethren, now that Jacob was dead, fearing that Joseph would now take revenge for the old wrong (the selling of him, 37:28, the guilty fear, the conscience still uneasy, the suspicion that Joseph's kindness had been only for the father's sake); the brethren's fear — that Joseph, the father gone, would hate and requite them (the guilty conscience, fearing revenge, though Joseph had freely forgiven them, 45:5-15, the lingering guilt, the misjudging of Joseph's heart); 'will certainly requite us all the evil which we did unto him' (the brethren owning their evil, 37:28 — the conscience still accusing, the fear of just requital, the occasion of Joseph's renewed forgiveness, vv.19-21, the guilt that the gospel of free forgiveness alone can quiet) — Henry, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And when Joseph's brethren saw that their father was dead, they said, Joseph will peradventure hate us, and will certainly requite us all the evil which we did unto him
the brethren's fear of Joseph's revenge
the guilty conscience still accusing for the old wrong (37:28)
fearing revenge though Joseph had freely forgiven them (45:5-15)

schools.summary: The brethren sending a message in Jacob's name: 'and they sent a messenger unto Joseph, saying, Thy father did command before he died, saying' — the brethren, fearing Joseph (v.15), sending a message (or coming themselves, v.18) claiming that Jacob, before dying, had commanded Joseph to forgive them (the appeal to the father's authority, the seeking of forgiveness, whether Jacob truly so charged, or the brethren urged it as his likely wish, the witnesses differ); the brethren's message (sent through a messenger, or themselves) — the seeking of reconciliation (the fear driving them to appeal, the message of entreaty); 'Thy father did command before he died' (the appeal to Jacob's charge, that Joseph forgive them, whether Jacob actually so commanded, some hold he did, knowing the brethren's fear, others that the brethren reasonably inferred his wish, or even slightly stretched it in their fear, the plea grounded on the father's authority, the seeking of forgiveness, v.17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And they sent a messenger unto Joseph, saying, Thy father did command before he died, saying
the brethren sending a message in Jacob's name
the appeal to the father's authority (whether Jacob so charged, or the brethren inferred his wish)
the seeking of forgiveness and reconciliation (v.17)

schools.summary: The brethren's plea for forgiveness and Joseph's tears: 'so shall ye say unto Joseph, Forgive, I pray thee now, the trespass of thy brethren, and their sin; for they did unto thee evil: and now, we pray thee, forgive the trespass of the servants of the God of thy father. And Joseph wept when they spake unto him' — the brethren (in Jacob's name, and their own) pleading for forgiveness of their trespass and sin (the selling of Joseph, 37:28, the open confession of their evil, the earnest entreaty for pardon); the appeal — 'the servants of the God of thy father' (the brethren pleading as fellow-worshippers of the covenant God, appealing to Joseph's piety, that he forgive as God's servant, the ground of the plea, the common faith, the earnest petition); 'Joseph wept when they spake unto him' (Joseph weeping, grieved that his brethren still feared him, still doubted his forgiveness, the tears of wounded love, that they thought him capable of revenge after all his kindness, 45:5-15, the tenderness of Joseph, no thought of revenge, only grief at their distrust, the heart of true forgiveness, pained by their fear) — Clarke, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: Forgive, I pray thee now, the trespass of thy brethren, and their sin... forgive the trespass of the servants of the God of thy father. And Joseph wept when they spake unto him
the brethren's plea for forgiveness and Joseph's tears
the open confession of their evil, the appeal as servants of the covenant God (37:28)
Joseph's tears of wounded love, grieved they still feared him (45:5-15)

schools.summary: The brethren prostrating themselves before Joseph: 'and his brethren also went and fell down before his face; and they said, Behold, we be thy servants' — the brethren coming themselves and falling down before Joseph, offering to be his servants (slaves, the deep submission, the guilty fear, the prostration, a further, full fulfillment of Joseph's dreams, 37:7-10, the sheaves and stars bowing, the offer of bondage, as Judah had offered, 44:33); the brethren falling down (the deep submission, the dreams fulfilled, 37:7-10) — the humbling before Joseph (the guilty fear driving them low); 'Behold, we be thy servants' (the brethren offering to be Joseph's slaves, the full submission, the guilty fear, offering bondage to escape revenge, the occasion of Joseph's gracious reply, vv.19-21, the dreams fulfilled, the brethren bowing) — Gill.

themes: And his brethren also went and fell down before his face; and they said, Behold, we be thy servants
the brethren prostrating themselves before Joseph
the deep submission, the dreams fully fulfilled (37:7-10)
the guilty fear offering bondage (as Judah, 44:33)

schools.summary: Joseph refusing the place of God: 'and Joseph said unto them, Fear not: for am I in the place of God?' — Joseph reassuring his brethren (against their guilty fear, v.15, the gentle comfort, the free forgiveness, no thought of revenge, the calming of their fear); Joseph's word — 'am I in the place of God?': Joseph disclaiming the place of God — meaning either (a) 'Am I to act as God, to take to myself the vengeance and judgment that belong to God alone?' (Joseph would not usurp God's prerogative of requital, Deuteronomy 32:35; Romans 12:19, 'Vengeance is mine; I will repay, saith the Lord'); or (b) 'Am I in God's stead, to oppose what God has done?' — God having overruled their evil for good (v.20), Joseph would not resist God's purpose (the witnesses noting both senses join: Joseph leaving vengeance to God, not usurping his place, and submitting to God's overruling providence, which turned the evil to good, the meekness that forgives, leaving judgment to God, the godly refusal of revenge); Joseph's 'am I in the place of God?' — the model of forgiveness (leaving vengeance to God, Romans 12:19, the meek heart that requites not evil for evil, 1 Peter 3:9, the example of the godly, and a type of Christ, who forgave his murderers, Luke 23:34, the refusal to avenge) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What does Joseph's 'am I in the place of God?' mean?

sides.position: Joseph refusing to usurp the vengeance and judgment that belong to God alone (Deuteronomy 32:35; Romans 12:19) - the meekness that forgives, leaving judgment to God, a type of Christ forgiving his murderers (Luke 23:34)
'Am I in God's stead, to oppose what God has done?' - Joseph submitting to God's overruling providence, which turned their evil to good (v.20), not resisting God's purpose

themes: And Joseph said unto them, Fear not: for am I in the place of God?
Joseph refusing the place of God (the crux of vengeance left to God)
refusing to usurp God's vengeance and judgment (Deuteronomy 32:35; Romans 12:19)
the meekness that forgives, a type of Christ forgiving his murderers (Luke 23:34)

schools.summary: The great providence - evil meant for good: 'but as for you, ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good, to bring to pass, as it is this day, to save much people alive' — Joseph acknowledging the brethren's real evil intent (the selling of him, 37:28, was truly their wicked deed, meant for harm, the honest naming of their sin, not excusing it, the real guilt owned); the great word — 'but God meant it unto good': the same act that the brethren intended for evil, God intended (meant, purposed) for good (the providence of God overruling the evil of men for good — not that God did the evil, the brethren's sin was truly theirs, freely and wickedly done, nor that the evil was good, but that God, in his secret, sovereign providence, directed and overruled their evil to accomplish his good purpose, the preservation of the family and the nations, the harmonizing of human responsibility and divine sovereignty, man's wicked intent and God's good purpose in the one act, as supremely in the cross, Acts 2:23, 'him, being delivered by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God, ye have... slain'; 4:27-28, the deep truth of providence, the great lesson of the whole Joseph history, and indeed of Genesis — God bringing good out of evil, ruling the sins of men to his own ends without being the author of them, the comfort to the afflicted, God overruling all for good, Romans 8:28); 'to bring to pass, as it is this day, to save much people alive' (God's good purpose: the saving of many, Egypt, the nations, and Jacob's house, from the famine, 45:5-7, the preservation of the covenant family, from which the Messiah would come, the providence that turned the brethren's crime into the means of salvation, the good God brought out of the evil) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: How does 'ye thought evil... but God meant it unto good' relate human sin and divine sovereignty?

sides.position: The same act the brethren truly and wickedly intended for harm (37:28), God sovereignly purposed and overruled for good - not the author of the evil, yet ruling it to his ends; the harmonizing of human guilt and divine sovereignty (as in the cross, Acts 2:23; 4:27-28), God bringing good out of evil to save much people alive (Romans 8:28)

themes: But as for you, ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good, to bring to pass, as it is this day, to save much people alive
the great providence - evil meant for good (the crux of the whole Joseph story)
the same act the brethren meant for evil (37:28), God sovereignly overruled for good - not the author of the evil
the harmonizing of human guilt and divine sovereignty (as in the cross, Acts 2:23; Romans 8:28), to save much people alive (45:5-7)

schools.summary: Joseph comforting his brethren and promising to provide: 'now therefore fear ye not: I will nourish you, and your little ones. And he comforted them, and spake kindly unto them' — Joseph, having owned the providence (v.20), reassuring his brethren: he would not avenge, but nourish (feed, provide for) them and their children (the free forgiveness sealed with kindness, the promise of provision, as in the famine, 45:11, the comfort); Joseph 'comforted them' (calmed their fears, reassured their guilty hearts) — the gentle comfort (the free forgiveness, the heart that requited evil with good, Romans 12:21); 'spake kindly unto them' (Hebrew, 'spake to their heart') — Joseph's tender, kind words (reassuring, affectionate, the heart-comfort, the model of forgiveness, the wronged comforting the wrongdoers, overcoming evil with good, the type of Christ comforting his erring people) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Now therefore fear ye not: I will nourish you, and your little ones. And he comforted them, and spake kindly unto them
Joseph comforting his brethren and promising to provide
the free forgiveness sealed with provision and kindness (45:11)
the wronged comforting the wrongdoers, overcoming evil with good (Romans 12:21)

schools.summary: Joseph's long life in Egypt: 'and Joseph dwelt in Egypt, he, and his father's house: and Joseph lived an hundred and ten years' — Joseph and the family of Israel dwelling in Egypt (the sojourn continuing, Joseph living to a hundred and ten years, the full age, esteemed by the Egyptians the ideal life-span, the long life of the godly ruler, the close of Joseph's days approaching); Joseph's age (a hundred and ten, the Egyptians' ideal age) — the full life (the providence prolonging Joseph's days, to govern, and to see his seed, v.23, the long life of the faithful); Joseph dwelling in Egypt with the family — the sojourn (the covenant family in Egypt until the Exodus, the providence, the seventy grown into a people, Exodus 1:7, the long life of Joseph closing the patriarchal age) — Henry, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Joseph dwelt in Egypt, he, and his father's house: and Joseph lived an hundred and ten years
Joseph's long life in Egypt
the full age of the godly ruler (a hundred and ten, the Egyptians' ideal)
the sojourn continuing until the Exodus (Exodus 1:7)

schools.summary: Joseph seeing his children's children: 'and Joseph saw Ephraim's children of the third generation: the children also of Machir the son of Manasseh were brought up upon Joseph's knees' — Joseph living to see his descendants to the third (or fourth) generation (Ephraim's great-grandchildren, and Machir's children, of Manasseh, the blessing of long life and a numerous seed, the children 'brought up upon Joseph's knees,' adopted, or fondled, as his own, the blessing of seeing his seed, Psalm 128:6); Joseph seeing his descendants to the third generation (the blessing of a numerous posterity, the fulfillment of the blessing on Ephraim, 48:19) — the seed multiplied (the long life rewarded); 'the children also of Machir... were brought up upon Joseph's knees' (Machir's children, of Manasseh, brought up on Joseph's knees, the token of adoption or fond care, the blessing of the godly old age, seeing the seed, Psalm 128:6, the providence, the seed growing toward the nation, Exodus 1:7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Joseph saw Ephraim's children of the third generation: the children also of Machir the son of Manasseh were brought up upon Joseph's knees
Joseph seeing his children's children
the blessing of long life and a numerous seed (48:19; Psalm 128:6)
the seed growing toward the nation (Exodus 1:7)

schools.summary: Joseph's dying faith in the visitation and the return: 'and Joseph said unto his brethren, I die: and God will surely visit you, and bring you out of this land unto the land which he sware to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob' — Joseph, dying, assuring his brethren that God would surely 'visit' them (come to them in mercy, to deliver) and bring them out of Egypt (the dying prophecy, the faith in the Exodus, foretold, 15:13-16, the certainty of God's deliverance); Joseph's dying faith — 'bring you out of this land unto the land which he sware to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob': Joseph, by faith, foreseeing and foretelling the Exodus (the bringing out of Egypt to Canaan, the land of the covenant oath, 15:18; 26:3; 28:13, the faith that looked beyond Egypt to the promised land, Egypt not their home, the dying testimony to the covenant promise, the faith specially commended — 'By faith Joseph, when he died, made mention of the departing of the children of Israel; and gave commandment concerning his bones,' Hebrews 11:22, the patriarch's hope handed on, the very promise on which the next book, Exodus, opens); 'the land which he sware to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob' (the covenant land, the oath to the fathers, 15:18; 17:8; 26:3; 28:13, the inheritance promised, the faith that held the oath sure, though Egypt was their present home, the dying hope in the covenant, the bones to be carried up, v.25) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What does Joseph's dying word 'God will surely visit you' display?

sides.position: Joseph's faith foreseeing the Exodus (15:13-16) and the return to the covenant land sworn to the fathers (15:18; 28:13) - the faith specially commended (Hebrews 11:22), looking beyond Egypt to the promised inheritance, the hope on which the book of Exodus opens

themes: And Joseph said unto his brethren, I die: and God will surely visit you, and bring you out of this land unto the land which he sware to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob
Joseph's dying faith in the visitation and the return (the crux of the dying hope)
the faith foreseeing the Exodus (15:13-16) and the return to the covenant land (15:18; 28:13)
the faith specially commended (Hebrews 11:22), the hope on which Exodus opens

schools.summary: Joseph's oath concerning his bones: 'and Joseph took an oath of the children of Israel, saying, God will surely visit you, and ye shall carry up my bones from hence' — Joseph making the children of Israel swear that, when God visited them and brought them out (the Exodus), they would carry his bones up out of Egypt (to be buried in Canaan, the dying charge, the faith in the return, the bones a pledge of the promised inheritance); the repeated assurance ('God will surely visit you') — Joseph's certain faith in the Exodus (15:13-16, the ground of the oath, the sure deliverance, the dying confidence); 'ye shall carry up my bones from hence' (Joseph's charge that his bones be carried to Canaan, fulfilled, the bones carried at the Exodus, Exodus 13:19, and buried at Shechem, Joshua 24:32, the faith that would not rest in Egypt, the embalmed body a standing witness, awaiting the Exodus, the pledge of the promised land, the dying patriarch's hope, Hebrews 11:22, the bones a perpetual testimony to Israel that Egypt was not their home) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Joseph took an oath of the children of Israel, saying, God will surely visit you, and ye shall carry up my bones from hence
Joseph's oath concerning his bones
the certain faith in the Exodus and the return (15:13-16)
the bones a standing pledge of the promised inheritance (Exodus 13:19; Joshua 24:32; Hebrews 11:22)

schools.summary: The death of Joseph, the close of Genesis: 'so Joseph died, being an hundred and ten years old: and they embalmed him, and he was put in a coffin in Egypt' — Joseph dying at a hundred and ten (the full age, v.22), and being embalmed (the Egyptian custom, as Jacob, v.2, the death of the godly ruler, the close of the patriarchal age, the end of the generation that went down to Egypt); the embalming (as Jacob, v.2) — the body preserved (for the future carrying up, v.25, the Egyptian honor, the body kept against the Exodus); the striking ending — 'he was put in a coffin in Egypt': Joseph's embalmed body laid in a coffin (an ark, or chest) in Egypt — not buried in Canaan (like Jacob), but kept, the coffin a standing pledge and witness (the book of Genesis, which opened with creation and the garden, closing with a coffin in Egypt — the reign of death, the wages of sin, Romans 6:23, over even the best, yet the coffin 'in Egypt' not the end, but a pledge of the Exodus, the bones to be carried up, v.25; Exodus 13:19; Joshua 24:32, the faith that looked beyond the coffin to the promised land, and beyond death to the resurrection, the unburied coffin a perpetual sermon to Israel — that Egypt was not their home, that God would surely visit them, the hope that bridges Genesis to Exodus, the bondage and the deliverance to come) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

debates.question: What does Genesis closing with 'a coffin in Egypt' display?

sides.position: The reign of death over even the godly (Romans 6:23), yet the coffin a standing pledge of the Exodus (the bones to be carried up, v.25; Exodus 13:19; Joshua 24:32) - the faith looking beyond the coffin to the promised land and the resurrection, the unburied coffin a witness that Egypt was not Israel's home, the hope bridging Genesis to Exodus

themes: So Joseph died, being an hundred and ten years old: and they embalmed him, and he was put in a coffin in Egypt
the death of Joseph, the close of Genesis (the crux of the unburied coffin)
Genesis closing with 'a coffin in Egypt' - the reign of death over even the godly (Romans 6:23)
yet the coffin a standing pledge of the Exodus, the faith looking beyond to the promised land (v.25; Hebrews 11:22)
